experiences.intentions: Key state: 4x4 board with highest tile 64 at row2 col2, six empty spaces, and bottom row/left side has two adjacent 8s vertically in col1. Goal in 2048 is preserve space and create merges while keeping high tiles organized. Candidate actions: Left immediately merges the two 8s in row 4? No, row4 [8,0,0,0] unchanged; row3 [8,4,0,0] unchanged, so no merge and little gain. Right scatters tiles and moves the 64 away from left structure. Up makes col4 [0,2,0,0]->[2,0,0,0] and generally compresses upward but creates fewer immediate gains. Down is strongest: column1 [2,16,8,8] becomes [2,16,16]? More precisely compress down to [0,2,16,16], creating an immediate 8+8 merge into 16 at the bottom while preserving many empty spaces. This increases tile value and keeps board reasonably ordered. Choose down.

Goal is to preserve mobility while setting up merges and avoiding a move that needlessly breaks structure. Candidate actions: left gives immediate merges on row 4 (4,2,0,0 unchanged) and row 3 shifts 32 left but no merge; right shifts many tiles but disperses the large tile area; down moves the 64 downward and may weaken positioning. Up keeps the 64 high, maintains empties, and avoids pushing large tiles into messy lower rows.

experiences.intentions: Key state: only 1 empty cell at row 3 col 1, so survival depends on making a move that both changes the board and ideally creates space. Highest tile 64 is at row 2 col 2; no immediate large merge is available. Candidate moves: up changes little and leaves no empty cells, risking a stuck board. Down also mostly compresses and likely leaves no useful space. Right moves the single empty to the left side but creates no merge. Left is best because row 1 merges 2+2 into 4 and row 3 shifts 2,32,8 left, creating at least one empty space; it improves board flexibility and preserves the 64. Goal now is survival and space creation, so pursue the only immediate merge/space-generating action: left.

experiences.intentions: Key state: only one empty cell at row 3 col 4; highest tile 64 at row 2 col 2. Need a move that is legal, creates space, and ideally preserves larger tiles. Candidate moves: Up merges the top row 4+4 and 2+2, also merges the third column 8+8, creating multiple empties and strong consolidation. Down also gives some merges but shifts the 64 downward and is less stable. Left mainly merges row 1 but compresses 64 left without as much benefit. Right merges row 1 and row 3 but moves big tiles toward the right edge awkwardly. Best sub-goal is maximize empty spaces and consolidation while keeping the 64 in a manageable position; Up does this best.

experiences.intentions: Key state: 4x4 2048 board with only two empty spaces at bottom-left and bottom-right. Highest tile 64 is at row 2 col 2, and there are useful merges available: row1 has 2+2 if moved right/left, row2 has 16+16, bottom row has 0,8,2,0. Goal: preserve space while creating strong merges and avoiding a full board with no moves. Candidate actions: Up likely packs tiles but creates no immediate merges in key rows and may reduce flexibility. Down also seems to create little merging and may disturb structure. Left would merge the top-row 2s and second-row 16s, but it fills the bottom-left with 8 and leaves only one empty, reducing space. Right merges the top-row 2s and second-row 16s as well, and also keeps the bottom-left empty after shifting bottom row to the right, preserving more maneuverability. So right best improves board while keeping an empty corner.

Comparing actions: left merges 64+64 immediately and also merges the bottom row's trailing zeros safely, keeping higher tiles toward the left; right also merges 64+64 but shifts large tiles to the right and may weaken future monotonic structure. Up/down do not merge the 64s and risk reducing mobility on a tight board. Best sub-goal now is immediate high-value merge with stable positioning, so choose left.
experiences.tasks: Achieve the highest possible tile in 2048 by merging tiles strategically.

experiences.intentions: Key state: 128 is at row 3 col 2 with bottom two rows nearly full and three empties concentrated in the top-right area. Goal is to preserve the high tile and create merges while avoiding a disruptive move that shifts the 128 away from its stable region. Left would merge the top row (4,2,4,0 stays similar) and second row becomes 2,8,0,0 with no gain; it also keeps board cramped. Right creates an immediate merge on the top row (4,2,4,0 -> 0,4,2,4? actually no merge) and on the second row shifts to [0,0,2,8]; more importantly it preserves the bottom stable rows and increases empty space on the left, which is safer for future play than up/left. So right best maintains the high tile while opening the board.

experiences.intentions: Key state: highest tile 128 is at row 3 col 2, but the board is quite full with only 3 empty spaces, so preserving mobility matters. Goal: make a merge or at least compress tiles to keep options open while not scattering the board. Candidate actions: Up merges the two 4s in the rightmost column and also consolidates top empties, improving space. Down creates no merges and pulls high tiles away from the top cluster. Left creates no immediate merges and leaves the right side fragmented. Right creates no immediate merges and shifts the 128 away from a more stable left-side structure. Best sub-goal is to gain space via an immediate merge, so choose up.

Immediate merges available are strongest horizontally on top row: [16,2,8,8] can merge the two 8s by moving left or right. Other rows/cols have no major instant merges. Goal is to improve board while maintaining empties and avoiding a clogging move; keep larger tiles relatively anchored. Candidate actions: left merges top-row 8+8 into 16 and also compacts lower rows safely, creating an extra empty. Right also merges top-row 8+8 but shifts the 128 away from its current position and packs tiles toward the right. Up creates no merge and fills the board more. Down creates no useful merge and risks disorder. Best sub-goal now is gain a merge and space with minimal disruption, so choose left.

experiences.intentions: Key state: 128 is at row 2 col 2 with a fairly crowded board and 3 empty spaces. Goal is to preserve mobility while setting up merges and ideally keeping the highest tile anchored. Evaluating moves: up creates only one empty and shifts the 128 upward, reducing stability; down also moves the 128 and does not create useful merges; right moves the 128 off its strong central-left position and gives limited merge value. Left produces immediate merges on rows 1 and 4 (16+? no, but row4 has 0 compaction and row1 compacts; crucially row4 [2,8,0,2] -> [2,8,2,0], row3 [32,4,2,0] stable) and keeps the 128 in place while maintaining 3 empties and a generally smoother high-value cluster on the left. Best sub-goal now is maintain board control and avoid dislodging 128, so choose left.

Checking moves: Up appears invalid since every column is blocked with no merges. Down is valid and creates a merge in the last column (2+2 -> 4), reducing empties to one and may worsen mobility. Goal: maximize future mobility and set up larger-tile structure while taking available safe merge. Between down/right, right keeps the strong 128 and 32 near upper rows and opens space on the left less effectively; down consolidates bottom and preserves left-heavy structure with highest tile not displaced. Sub-goal now is to take the available merge with best future mobility. Choose down.

Goal: keep board open while setting up future merges around the high tile; avoid moves that likely reduce empties or trap the 128. Candidate actions: Up compresses columns and creates a merge in column 4 (16+2? no), but moves high tile to top and may clutter. Down keeps high tile lower but creates no immediate merges and can jam board. Left creates an immediate merge in top row (4+? no) and second row (2+128 no), but shifts 128 away from edge support; likely no merges except moving empties right. Right gives an immediate merge in column/row structure: top row [0,4,16,4]->[0,4,16,4], second [0,2,128,16]->same, third [32,4,32,2]->same, fourth same, so little change. Checking Up more carefully: col1 [0,0,32,2]->[32,2,0,0], col2 [4,2,4,8] no merge, col3 [16,128,32,4] no merge, col4 [4,16,2,8] no merge. Left: top [0,4,16,4]->[4,16,4,0], second [0,2,128,16]->[2,128,16,0], others unchanged, yielding two empties on right edge and keeping structure. This improves mobility more than other moves without breaking tiles. Best sub-goal is maximize open space; choose left.

Goal is to maximize survival and create new space/merge opportunities; down best advances that compared with up, left, or right.

experiences.intentions: Board is nearly full with only one empty space at top-right. The 128 tile is at row 3 col 3, and there are no immediate merges available on the current board in any row/column except moves that create space. Goal is to preserve mobility and ideally keep larger tiles stable while maximizing empty spaces. Up and down appear to leave the board unchanged because each column is already fully packed with no equal adjacent tiles, so they are likely invalid/no-op moves. Left shifts the top row [8,16,2,0] to [8,16,2,0], also likely no change overall. Right changes the top row to [0,8,16,2], creating an actual move and preserving future mobility. Therefore right is the only effective action.

Checking rows/columns: left/right produce no adjacent equal tiles in any row, so likely no change. Up/down each allow a merge in the last column (2,2,4,32), combining the top two 2s. Goal is to survive by making a legal move and ideally preserve the 128 tile while opening space. Between up and down, up creates the merge at the top and leaves an empty cell lower in the column, which is as good as any available option; left/right are dead, and down is similar. I choose up.
